claim: AD researcher Markku Kurkinen publicly argues lecanemab is 'not the right drug' - citing no slowing of decline in women and enhanced decline in APOE4 homozygotes - and questions whether its approval was the FDA's worst since aducanumab. A named-expert skeptic signal on the anti-amyloid class.
venue: Editorial, Advances in Clinical and Experimental Medicine
attribution: Kurkinen M, single-author editorial (named expert on record)
quote: In this 18-month trial, lecanemab did not slow cognitive decline in women. ... Lecanemab did not slow cognitive decline in APOE4 carriers; rather, it enhanced the decline in study participants with 2 APOE4 genes.
tier_note: Spec tiers a named-expert-on-record as T3; mapped to corpus T2 (gate is T1/T2). Anonymous/social (T4) is live-harvest only - see manifest.

claim: Mayo neurologist David Knopman (an FDA Peripheral & CNS Drugs Advisory Committee member who reviewed aducanumab) and Joel Perlmutter publicly framed aducanumab as having 'Meager Efficacy and Real Risks' - a high-profile named-KOL caution against prescribing it.
who: David S. Knopman (Mayo Clinic) & Joel S. Perlmutter (WashU)
attribution: Knopman DS, Perlmutter JS (named experts on record; Knopman was on the FDA AdComm)
quote: Prescribing Aducanumab in the Face of Meager Efficacy and Real Risks
tier_note: Verbatim editorial title (abstract not indexed) = the experts' on-record framing. Named-expert (spec T3) -> corpus T2.

claim: Howard Bauchner (former JAMA editor-in-chief) and G. Caleb Alexander (Johns Hopkins; an FDA AdComm member on aducanumab) characterized the medical community's response as a 'Rejection of Aducanumab (Aduhelm) by the Health Care Community' - signaling broad expert/institutional pushback.
who: Howard Bauchner (BU) & G. Caleb Alexander (Johns Hopkins)
attribution: Bauchner H, Alexander GC (named experts on record; Alexander was on the FDA AdComm)
quote: Rejection of Aducanumab (Aduhelm) by the Health Care Community: Lessons Learned and the Path Ahead
tier_note: Verbatim editorial title. Named-expert (spec T3) -> corpus T2.

claim: Schizophrenia neuroscientist Daniel Javitt (Columbia/Nathan Kline) framed the muscarinic agent xanomeline-trospium (KarXT/Cobenfy) for cognition with measured optimism - 'Hope for Some, or Hope for All?' - signaling cautious KOL enthusiasm for the class beyond positive symptoms.
who: Daniel C. Javitt (Columbia / Nathan Kline Institute)
attribution: Javitt DC, single-author editorial (leading schizophrenia KOL)
quote: Xanomeline-Trospium Treatment of Cognitive Impairments of Schizophrenia: Hope for Some, or Hope for All?
tier_note: Verbatim editorial title (the KOL's on-record framing). Named-expert (spec T3) -> corpus T2.

claim: Commentators frame Cobenfy (xanomeline-trospium) as a first-in-class, dopamine-sparing antipsychotic 'marking a significant advancement in schizophrenia treatment' and 'a promising alternative,' especially for treatment-resistant or intolerant patients - the optimistic pole of muscarinic-class sentiment.
who: Abdul Haseeb Hasan & Muhammad Ali Abid (Mayo Hospital, Lahore)
attribution: Hasan AH, Abid MA (named, but not senior KOLs - weight accordingly)
quote: Cobenfy, a novel antipsychotic combining xanomeline and trospium chloride, received the United States (US) Food and Drug Administration (FDA) approval, marking a significant advancement in schizophrenia treatment.
tier_note: Named authors but junior (not senior KOLs); included as a sentiment data-point, down-weighted per the spec.

claim: EMET validation of the KOL skeptic claim (card #1): a 2026 systematic review/meta-analysis (6 RCTs, N=5633) confirms anti-amyloid efficacy varies by subgroup - greatest in ApoE4 non-carriers, with sex among the modifiers - and ARIA risk higher in ApoE4 carriers (homozygotes most). The subgroup concern is real, but CLARITY AD was not powered for a sex x treatment interaction, so 'no benefit in women' overreads an underpowered subgroup.
attribution: Shim GH et al. 2025 - via EMET
quote: Both lecanemab and donanemab showed the greatest slowing of cognitive decline in White/Caucasian patients and apolipoprotein E4 (ApoE4) non-carriers... The efficacy and safety of lecanemab and donanemab varied across patient subgroups, including age, sex, race/ethnicity and genetic factors such as ApoE4 genotype status.
